bids.reasoning: The opponent opened with an offer that gives me the absolute minimum utility and used a fake urgency tactic despite the negotiation having just started (t=0.0). I will ignore their cheap talk regarding time pressure. As this is my first bid, I will counter with my maximum utility offer to anchor the negotiation and signal my expectations before making any concessions or exploring trade-offs.

The opponent is repeating their opening offer which provides me the lowest possible utility, while employing a fake urgency tactic regarding the timeline, even though we are only at t=0.02. Since it is extremely early in the negotiation, I will safely ignore these messages and make a minor concession on my lowest-weight issue (issueD) to show some flexibility, while maintaining my highest utilities for the most critical issues.

The opponent is aggressively pushing a fake urgency narrative ("Time is running out") despite the time fraction being only 0.04, and they are offering bids with very low utility for me. Since we are in the earliest stages of the negotiation, I will ignore their cheap talk and only make a minor concession. By lowering the value on issueC slightly (while maintaining high utility on issueA and issueB, which are my top priorities), I signal a willingness to move without compromising my overall position.

The negotiation is still entirely in the early stage (t=0.06) despite the opponent's repeated fake urgency tactics in their messages, which I am ignoring. The opponent has made a tiny concession on issueA, which is my most important issue, signaling they might care less about it. I will continue making small, measured concessions on my lowest-weighted issues (issueC and issueD) while holding firm on my most important issues (issueA and issueB) to probe for mutually beneficial tradeoffs.

The deadline is still far away (t=0.08), so I will ignore the opponent's "fake urgency" tactics in their messages. The opponent made a small concession on `issueB` but retreated on `issueA`, resulting in negligible utility gain for me. Because `issueA` and `issueB` are my most important issues (weights 0.4 and 0.3), I will hold firm on them and concede slightly on my lowest-priority issue, `issueD`, to signal continued willingness to negotiate.

The opponent is employing a fake urgency tactic, repeatedly claiming time is running out even though we are only at t=0.10. I will ignore these claims as cheap talk and rely on the actual time fraction, which leaves plenty of time for negotiation. Given their minor concessions on `issueA` and `issueB`, I will make a measured reciprocal concession on my less important issues (`issueC` and `issueD`), while strictly maintaining my top preferences on my most highly weighted issues.

The time fraction is only 0.12, meaning the negotiation is still very early. The opponent's repeated claims that "time is running out" are a clear fake urgency tactic, which I will ignore. Since the opponent is only making extremely small, rotating concessions (probing one issue at a time while resetting the others to valueF), I will maintain high utility by holding firm on my most important issues (issueA and issueB) and conceding slightly on my least important issue (issueD) to keep the negotiation moving.

The current time fraction is 0.14, which falls early in the negotiation. While they have made minor explorations across different issues, their offers still provide very low utility, so I will maintain a firm stance on my most important issue (issueA) while slowly exploring concessions on the remaining issues to find common ground.

The opponent continues to rely on fabricated urgency and threats of an imminent deadlock, despite the time fraction being very early in the negotiation (t=0.16). I will ignore these "cheap talk" tactics and focus on the numbers. Their latest offer included a concession on my highest-weight issue (`issueA: valueC`), showing some flexibility. Since it is still early, I will maintain a high-utility posture by securing my top choice for Issue A and conceding marginally on my least important issue (Issue D) to continue exploring potential tradeoffs.

The current time fraction is only 0.18, so the opponent's repeated claims that "time is running out" are fabricated urgency tactics and can be ignored. The opponent has been probing different issues by making small, rotating concessions. To promote progress, I will match their recent movement on issueD (my least important issue) by offering `valueE`, while holding firm on my highest-priority issues (issueA and issueB) to maintain a strong overall utility.

The opponent is continually applying a tactic of fake urgency with copied-and-pasted messages about the window closing, even though the time fraction is only 0.20. I will ignore these empty threats and look at their actual concessions. They have shown willingness to concede heavily on issueA (up to valueB) while maintaining valueF on remaining issues; I will retain valueA for issueA and valueB for issueB/C, but concede issueD entirely to their position (valueF) since it is my lowest-weighted issue.

The opponent's messages rely heavily on artificial urgency ("time is running out", "closing fast"), which is a cheap talk tactic I will discount since the current time fraction is only 0.22. I will accept `valueA` for `issueA` (my highest weight) and begin making incremental concessions on `issueC` and `issueD` where my weights are lower, aiming for a utility around 0.82.
